nativeRange.habitat: Clear lagoon and seaward coral reefs, typically encountered in pairs or small groups at 1–25 m depth

diet.notes: Reported in the wild to graze on substrate, gastropods, and eggs, alongside small benthic invertebrates and some algae. In aquaria, generally accepts a varied omnivore diet of mysis, brine, finely chopped seafood (shrimp, mussel, clam, squid), quality marine pellets, and vegetable-based preparations including nori and marine-algae blends. Several small meals per day are generally advised; some individuals are initially shy feeders and may need coaxing with whole or live foods before transitioning to prepared diets.

goodWith: small to medium peaceful reef fish

summary: The Mimic Filefish is a small Indo-Pacific species best known for closely resembling the toxic Valentin's sharpnose puffer (Canthigaster valentini), a form of protective mimicry that deters predators. Generally peaceful, it is not considered reef-safe and has been reported to nip at sessile invertebrates including corals and sponges.
careNotes:
The Mimic Filefish, also sold as the Blacksaddle Filefish, Black-Saddle Mimic Filefish, or Saddled Filefish, is a small member of the family Monacanthidae (filefish) from Indo-Pacific coral reefs. It is best known for Batesian mimicry — a form of mimicry where a harmless species closely resembles a dangerous one — of the toxic Valentin's sharpnose puffer, Canthigaster valentini. The two species share nearly identical coloration and patterning, and the filefish gains protection from predators by impersonating the puffer in areas where both occur. They are most easily told apart by body shape: the filefish has two dorsal fins (a short spine near the head and a soft-rayed fin set far back along the body) and a rough, file-like skin texture, while the puffer has a single dorsal fin and a rounder, smoother body. DO NOT confuse the two in the trade — their care requirements differ substantially, and Canthigaster valentini produces tetrodotoxin, a potent toxin found in its tissues.

Reef compatibility is the species' main practical caveat. While generally calmer and less destructive than many larger filefish, the Mimic Filefish is not considered reef-safe. It has been reported to nip at a broad range of sessile invertebrates (animals that remain fixed in place, such as soft corals, zoanthids, gorgonians, sponges, tunicates, and feather dusters), as well as small ornamental shrimp and snails. A fish-only-with-live-rock (FOWLR) system is the most commonly recommended setup; a reef tank stocked exclusively with stony corals may be trialled by experienced keepers, but close monitoring is advised and the fish should be removed at the first sign of nipping.

In the wild, Mimic Filefish are typically encountered in pairs or small groups and are understood to form monogamous bonds. Mated pairs are commonly kept together in captivity, but two individuals that have not already paired should not be housed together. Keep only one specimen or one established pair per tank, and do not mix with other filefish. Avoid housing alongside the Valentin's puffer — compatibility tends to break down at close quarters, and their visual similarity can cause confusion. Provide ample rockwork with caves and overhangs for shelter, along with open swimming space; despite being relatively calm swimmers, the species benefits from a tank with a generous footprint.
breedingNotes: Wild pairs are reported to spawn within defended territories on the reef, but the species has not been reliably bred in home aquaria. The trade is overwhelmingly wild-caught specimens.

primary.name: FishBase
primary.notes: Used as primary because the assigned LiveAquaria product page (https://www.liveaquaria.com/products/mimic-filefish) returned HTTP 404 and a search on LiveAquaria's site surfaced no current product listing for this species. FishBase confirmed scientific name Paraluteres prionurus (Bleeker, 1851), family Monacanthidae, order Tetraodontiformes, max 11.0 cm TL, depth 1–25 m, marine reef-associated/non-migratory, Indo-Pacific distribution (East Africa to Fiji, north to southern Japan, south to southern GBR and New Caledonia), wild temperature 25.2–29.3°C, diet of substrate grazing on gastropods and eggs, IUCN Least Concern (assessed 2015).

additional.name: AquaticCommunity
additional.notes: Promoted to secondary after the assigned LiveAquaria primary 404'd. Cross-confirmed husbandry: max 4.5 in (~11 cm), minimum 30 US gal (~120 L) suitable for a single specimen or pair, temperature 72–78°F (22–26°C), pH 8.1–8.4, SG 1.020–1.025, omnivore diet of chopped seafood plus herbivore preparations, peaceful temperament, NOT reef-safe (eats sponges, tunicates, gorgonians, snails, shrimp), wild pairs in defended territories, no established captive breeding, Batesian mimicry of Canthigaster valentini.

additional.name: Fishkeeper
additional.notes: Second secondary; UK aquarium retailer/care site. Confirmed 10 cm max, 22–28°C, pH 8.1–8.4, SG 1.020–1.025, omnivore (krill/brineshrimp/chopped seafood plus nori/algae), 3 small feedings daily, docile/passive, kept singly or as a mated pair, NOT reef-safe (risk to sessile invertebrates), explicit incompatibility with Valentin's puffer (the mimicked species).

additional.name: Reef Life Survey
additional.notes: Wikipedia used only to corroborate taxonomic background and Batesian-mimicry biology of Canthigaster valentini; not used for husbandry figures. Confirmed family Monacanthidae, max 11 cm, Indo-Pacific reef habitat in pairs or small schools, Bleeker 1851 description, two-dorsal-fin vs one-dorsal-fin distinction from the mimicked puffer.

fish.breedingNotes: Wild pairs spawn within defended territories on the reef. No established captive-breeding protocol; the trade is essentially all wild-caught specimens.
